skill.name: nanobanana
skill.description: Generate, edit, restore, and format images using Google Gemini image models through a local Python CLI.
skill.summary: Create Gemini-powered images, icons, patterns, diagrams, story frames, edits, and restorations from natural language prompts.
skill.author: batou9150

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but manual review shows no confirmed malicious intent and no prompt injection attempts. The main real risks are expected for this skill type: environment API key access, external Gemini API calls, local image file reads for edit and restore, automatic dependency bootstrap, and a documented pipe-to-shell uv installer command.

medium_findings.title: External Gemini API Use With Environment API Key
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally reads Gemini API keys from environment variables and sends prompts, and sometimes image bytes, to the Gemini API. This is expected for image generation, but users must understand that prompt and image content leave the local machine.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly obtains API keys from environment variables and creates a genai.Client. The model call passes prompt content and optional image bytes to the external Gemini service.

medium_findings.title: Local Image File Reads Can Upload User Files
medium_findings.description: The edit and restore flows resolve image filenames from the current directory, common subdirectories, Downloads, Desktop, or absolute paths, then read the file bytes for the model request. This is legitimate for image editing but can expose local image contents if a user selects the wrong file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file search and read paths are explicit and are used by image edit and restore operations. The behavior is expected, but the privacy risk is real when images are sent to a remote model.

medium_findings.title: Supply Chain Exposure From Installer and Runtime Dependency Bootstrap
medium_findings.description: The README recommends installing uv through a downloaded shell script, and the skill script relies on uv inline metadata to provision google-genai automatically. This is not malicious, but it adds supply chain trust requirements outside the skill repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command and automatic dependency provisioning are visible in the documentation and script header. These are common installation patterns, but users must trust the upstream installer and package source.

low_findings.title: Subprocess Preview Command Is Constrained
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged subprocess.Popen, but the command is selected from fixed platform preview commands and receives the generated path as a separate argument. I did not find evidence of shell=True or user-controlled command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call uses fixed command arrays for open, cmd start, or xdg-open. The path can vary, but it is not interpolated into a shell command.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, hidden home file, and hardcoded URL alerts are markdown examples, model names, prompt placeholders, or documentation paths. I did not find evidence that these markdown lines perform code execution or cryptographic operations at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown tables, fenced examples, environment variable lists, or model labels. The only runtime code reviewed is the Python script, where these specific markdown patterns are not executed.

low_findings.title: Base64 Decode Handles Gemini Image Response Data
low_findings.description: The base64 decode finding appears to be a safe response parsing pattern. It decodes inline image data returned by the Gemini SDK and writes the resulting bytes as image files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The decoded bytes come from image response parts and are saved as generated output. I did not find evidence of decoded code execution or hidden payload loading.

content.value_statement: Teams need usable visual assets without leaving their coding workflow. This skill connects Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Gemini image models for generation, editing, restoration, icons, patterns, stories, and diagrams.
actual_capabilities: Generates images from text prompts with optional counts, styles, and variation controls.
Edits or restores existing local image files using natural language instructions.
Creates app icons, favicons, and UI elements with configurable sizes and formats.
Generates seamless patterns, textures, wallpapers, and sequential story frames.
Creates flowcharts, architecture diagrams, wireframes, mind maps, and sequence diagrams.
Saves generated files under nanobanana-output and prints the saved paths.
limitations: Requires uv and a Gemini API key in a supported environment variable.
Sends prompts and selected image bytes to the external Gemini API.
Generated results depend on Gemini model availability, quota, and safety filtering.
Preview opening depends on the local operating system and desktop configuration.

use_cases.title: Design Asset Drafting
use_cases.description: Create icons, favicons, UI elements, patterns, and wallpapers for early product design work.

use_cases.title: Technical Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate architecture diagrams, flowcharts, database diagrams, and wireframes for technical documentation.

use_cases.title: Image Cleanup
use_cases.description: Edit existing images or restore damaged photos with direct text instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Create One Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of {subject} in a {style} style with {important details}.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this when you need one visual from a clear description.

prompt_templates.title: Explore Variations
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate {count} variations of {subject} using {styles} and variation type {variation}.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this when comparing visual directions before choosing one.

prompt_templates.title: Edit a Local Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit {file name} to {specific change}. Keep {elements to preserve} unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this when modifying an existing image from the workspace or input folders.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Diagram Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a {diagram type} diagram for {system} showing {components}, {data flow}, and {annotations level}.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this for technical diagrams with controlled layout and detail.

output_examples.input: Generate a modern app icon for a calendar tool in 256 and 512 pixel sizes.
output_examples.output: The skill saves two icon files in nanobanana-output and returns their file paths.

output_examples.input: Edit product-photo.png to place the mug on a clean white background.
output_examples.output: The skill reads the selected image, sends it with the edit prompt, and saves the edited image path.

output_examples.input: Create an architecture diagram for a web app with API, worker, cache, and database layers.
output_examples.output: The skill saves a generated diagram image and prints the path for use in documentation.

best_practices: Name the subject, style, output purpose, and any required sizes in the first request.
Use specialized modes for icons, patterns, diagrams, stories, edits, and restorations.
Confirm the exact local image path before asking the skill to edit or restore a file.
anti_patterns: Do not place private or regulated images in edit requests unless external API upload is approved.
Do not rely on vague prompts when brand, layout, text, or sizing must be precise.
Do not run installer commands from documentation without reviewing the upstream source first.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates images, edited photos, restorations, icons, patterns, story frames, and diagrams through Gemini image models.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It looks for NANOBANANA_API_KEY first, then supported Gemini and Google API key environment variables.

faq.question: Where are images saved?
faq.answer: Generated output is saved in a nanobanana-output directory under the current working directory.

faq.question: Does it send data outside my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. It sends prompts and selected image bytes to the Gemini API to create or edit images.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The reviewed behavior appears legitimate, but it uses external network calls, API keys, local file reads, and dependency bootstrap.
